Hi, Robert
I came across something I can’t ignore lately.
It feels like the world is getting more tense, more unstable, and what used to feel extreme is slowly becoming normal. Not in one place, but everywhere.
And at the same time, we’re more connected than ever.
I kept thinking about this contradiction for a long time and eventually wrote a book about it, One World: A Manual for Global Happiness.
The idea is simple, we already live as one system, but continue to act as separate parts, and that gap creates the pressure we all feel.
If you want to take a look:
t.co/B4IX8kO568
And if you’re not into supporting financially, that’s completely fine.
But I’d still be glad to hear your thoughts, or even start small, conversations, local groups, something real.
Feels like this shouldn’t stay just an idea.

Paid Collaboration

Hi,

Hope you're doing well!

5:38 PM
5:38 PM

Similar; same family of trust, different uses.  Homomorphic encryption lets a server perform arbitrary computation on encrypted data; it's powerful for things like search/ML inference on private data, but it’s slow and the schemes are still generally nonstandard. 
(Our) Zero-Exposure Signing (ZES) is much narrower: the server signs data it never sees, with near-zero overhead, using NIST-standardized post-quantum signatures under the hood. 

The way I think about it for agents is that you could reach for homomorphic when you need a server to do something to private data, and you'd reach for ZES when you need a server to attest to something private -  sign an audit log, prove a transaction happened, attest a chain of agent actions - all without leaking what the data actually was.
9:12 PM
9:12 PM

So, how to explain it to everyday developers or humans and get them to use. Hard problem.
10:23 PM
10:23 PM

Honestly, people don't want their agents getting hijacked. Signing actions you never authorized, sending things as you, calling APIs in your name? ZES is lightweight, is running NIST-standardized PQ signatures, real-time, at almost no cost, with free tiers, three-line install. (And you don't even have to trust CycleCore because we never actually receive your data.) It's current, government-approved math, low-friction, your actual data never leaves your machine. 

People need to at least try with security. In the day and age where we’ll-established shops are getting hacked, and quantum computer access is becoming more available the digital world is going to be a mess if we don't adopt and continue improving easy security upgrades. Production agents shouldn't be running unsigned actions in 2026. That's the PSA, lol. 

Especially now that anyone can spin up agentic tools (and more value is being placed online), attackers included

Most likely causes (in order):
1. Callback URL mismatch — The URL registered in X's Developer Portal doesn't exactly match what the app sends. X is strict — even a trailing slash breaks it.
2. Session not persisting across the OAuth redirect — The state parameter stored before redirecting to X isn't being read back correctly on return. Often a SameSite cookie issue.
3. User creation failing silently — X auth succeeds, but something fails when creating the account (database error, duplicate, missing field) and instead of showing an error, it loops back to login.

Tell the user: Yes, get the screen recording — it'll show exactly which URLs are looping, which narrows it down immediately.

​Syeda Nazifa Nawroj
Founder & Principal Attorney, Merger Lawyer

​Most founders build companies without fully understanding the legal infrastructure beneath them—until a financing, dispute, data-compliance issue, or acquisition forces the issue.

​In this interactive keynote, Silicon Valley M&A attorney Syeda Nazifa Nawroj will walk founders through the legal fundamentals that protect personal assets, reduce liability, and maximize the exit value of AI companies. The session will cover entity formation, founder roles and responsibilities, cap-table readiness, AI data compliance, the M&A lifecycle, and what buyers uncover during due diligence.

​Drawing from her experience advising on more than 35 transactions representing nearly $3 billion in deal value, Syeda will explain how founders can identify legal exposure early, negotiate from a position of strength, and prepare their companies for investment or acquisition.

Panelist #1: Robert Scoble — x.com/Scobleizer
Panelist #2: TBA

​The session will conclude with an interactive Q&A.

I will focus on how the platform can help influencers to grow their own brand portal, and own their own data and relationships, without just relying on Big Tech platform like X. The goal is to give every influencer, teacher, organizer their own branded portal and AI bots. The AI can act like a producer, do outreach book guests, etc.

As a side effect this can also help grow their channel on platforms like X, YouTube, Linkedin. But the idea is to post ONLY short form teaser content on there, for discovery and a link to your own site. The long form content and valuable connections should all be on your site that you control and can monetize.

Basically, launching your own community comes down to two major things: 1) content, and 2) connections. Influencers often have great contacts and this can help them unlock the power of their network, increase dealflow for certain projects, and monetize all this, automate with AI, while owning all the data inside the box.
